text: The surgeon applies a clip on the proximal section point identified through a suprapubic mini laparotomy. The coloepiploic detachment is performed towards the left colic angle, revealing the posterior wall of the stomach and confirming the opening of the lesser sac. The release of the trisangle allows for the identification of the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), with blunt dissection maneuvers used to expose the embryological plane between the Toldt's fascia and the dendrota fascia beneath the vein.

The surgeon applies a clip on the proximal section point identified through a suprapubic mini laparotomy. The coloepiploic detachment is directed towards the left colic angle, revealing the posterior wall of the stomach and confirming the opening of the lesser sac. The release of the triangular area allows for the identification of the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), with blunt dissection maneuvers performed in the embryological plane beneath the vein.

The surgeon performs dissection in a medium lateral direction within the subvascular plane, approaching the colic wall and the lower edge of the pancreas. The inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) is isolated and sectioned between clips, with its distal portion suspended upwards. The mesocolon is incised just above the pancreas to enter the lesser sac, highlighting and sectioning the root of the transverse mesocolon along the pancreatic border.

The surgeon performs dissection in a medium lateral direction within the subvascular plane, approaching the colic wall and the presumed origin of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) without exposing it. The inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) is isolated and sectioned between clips, with its distal portion suspended upwards. The mesocolon is incised just above the pancreas to access the lesser sac, highlighting and sectioning the root of the transverse mesocolon along the pancreatic border.

The surgeon completes the lateral mobilization of the colon upwards, reconnecting with the previously established dissection plane. The dissection reaches the splenic flexure, with the inferior mesenteric border (IMB) marking the medial limit. The aortic plane and the presumed origin of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) are visually identified.
The surgeon completes the lateral mobilization of the colon, reconnecting with the previously established dissection plane to facilitate the full mobilization of the splenic flexure.
The surgeon identifies the aortic plane and the presumed origin of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), with the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) constituting the medial limit of the dissection.

The surgeon identifies the sigmoid vascular branches and incises the mesocolon laterally to the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV). The dissection resumes the separation between the Toldt fascia and the Nigerota fascia.

The surgeon identifies the sigmoid vascular branches and incises the mesocolon laterally to the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV). The separation between the Toldt fascia and the Nigerota fascia, which was started earlier, is resumed.

The surgeon completes the lateral mobilization of the colon upwards, reconnecting with the previous dissection plane. The procedure continues downwards, freeing the sigmoid colon and incising the peritoneal reflection.

The surgeon completes the lateral mobilization of the colon upwards, reconnecting with the previous dissection plane. The procedure continues downwards, freeing the sigmoid colon and incising the peritoneal reflection.
The surgeon identifies the first sigmoid artery, coagulates it using an electrocautery device, and sections it, ensuring hemostasis in the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies, coagulates, and sections the first sigmoid artery. Intramesocolic dissection begins below the colonic wall, proceeding in contact with the bowel above the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The peritoneum is incised, marking the section line upwards and downwards to the upper third of the rectum. Sigmoid arterial branches are individually coagulated and sectioned.

The surgeon begins intramesocolic dissection below the colonic wall, maintaining contact with the bowel above the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The peritoneum is incised, marking the section line upwards and downwards to the upper third of the rectum. Sigmoid arterial branches are individually coagulated and sectioned.

The surgeon reaches the upper rectum, surrounding it from above and including the rectum-sigma junction in the resection. The section plane is maintained above the upper rectal artery, reaching the proximal rectum while leaving the mesorectum intact.

The surgeon reaches the upper rectum, surrounding it from above and including the rectum-sigma junction in the resection. The section plane is maintained above the upper rectal artery, reaching the proximal rectum while leaving the mesorectum intact.
The surgeon prepares the colon for distal sectioning below the rectum sigmoid junction, an area characterized by greater intraluminal pressure near the sphincter.
The surgeon prepares the colon for distal sectioning below the rectum sigmoid junction, an area characterized by greater intraluminal pressure near the sphincter.

The surgeon uses a mechanical stapler to transect the bowel, potentially requiring multiple refills. The bowel is then externalized through a suprapubic incision, with a clip marking the proximal section margin. A straight mechanical colorectal anastomosis is performed.

The surgeon uses a mechanical stapler to transect the bowel, occasionally requiring multiple refills. The bowel is then externalized through a suprapubic incision, with a clip marking the proximal section margin. A straight mechanical colorectal anastomosis is performed.

The surgeon achieves correct orientation of the colon axis and closes the mesocolic bridge using surgical glue. A mechanical stapler is fired to complete the anastomosis. The anastomosis is tested visually by transanal irrigation with methionine blue or the Ehrlich test, followed by perioperative rectoscopy to check for any anastomotic bleeding.
